subtopic_detail: Baseline metabolic workup before olanzapine initiation
stem: A 26-year-old man with a first episode of schizophrenia commenced olanzapine 10 mg nocte two weeks ago after baseline metabolic monitoring (BMI 24.3, waist 92 cm, BP 124/78, fasting glucose 4.8, lipids within range, ECG normal). His father has T2DM diagnosed at 58 and his mother has dyslipidaemia. He is a non-smoker, drinks two standard drinks on weekends, and is on no other medications. He has gained 1.8 kg in two weeks. The team plans to follow the RANZCP/Lambert metabolic-monitoring schedule for second-generation antipsychotics.

data_table.Observations: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76, weight 78 kg, BMI 24.3, waist 92 cm.
data_table.Bloods: Not yet taken at this visit.

lead_in: Per the RANZCP-endorsed metabolic monitoring schedule for second-generation antipsychotics, what monitoring should occur at the 12-week post-initiation review?

options.text: Weight and BMI only; bloods are scheduled at 6 months and annually thereafter
options.rationale: The RANZCP schedule explicitly includes fasting glucose (or HbA1c) and fasting lipids at 12 weeks, in addition to anthropometry and BP, because the highest-risk window for emergent metabolic disturbance is the first 3 months on olanzapine.

options.text: Weight, BMI, waist circumference, BP, fasting glucose (or HbA1c), and fasting lipids; repeat ECG only if symptoms or risk-factor change
options.rationale: At the 12-week timepoint the schedule mandates full anthropometric panel (weight, BMI, waist), BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and fasting lipids; ECG repetition is not routinely required at 12 weeks unless QTc prolongation risk has changed (new QTc-prolonging drug, electrolyte derangement, syncope). This is the highest-yield repeat-monitoring window because olanzapine-related weight gain and dyslipidaemia peak in the first 3-6 months.

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR in addition to anthropometry
options.rationale: OGTT, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are not part of the routine RANZCP schedule; fasting glucose or HbA1c plus fasting lipids are sufficient at 12 weeks. Insulin-resistance metrics are research/specialist-endocrine tools.

options.text: Repeat ECG, prolactin, and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure as a 12-week metabolic panel
options.rationale: These are not part of the metabolic monitoring schedule; prolactin is monitored only on prolactin-elevating antipsychotics (e.g. risperidone, amisulpride), 24-hour ABPM is not routine, and ECG at 12 weeks is only if indicated.

options.text: Defer all repeat metabolic bloods until the 12-month anniversary review
options.rationale: Anti-pattern: olanzapine carries the highest metabolic-burden signal among SGAs and the first 12 weeks is when emergent dyslipidaemia and impaired fasting glucose are most readily reversed by early switch; 12-month-only monitoring misses this window.

explanation.summary: Olanzapine carries the highest weight-gain and dysglycaemia signal among commonly used second-generation antipsychotics. RANZCP's baseline panel anchors later monitoring at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and annually (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
key_points: Baseline: weight, BMI, waist, BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, ECG
Follow-up: 6 wk, 12 wk, then annually (more often if abnormal)
Clozapine adds baseline echocardiogram, troponin, CRP
Switch consideration triggered by >7% weight gain or new metabolic syndrome criteria
explanation.pearls: Document baseline waist circumference - it is the earliest mover and the easiest to repeat (RANZCP).

stem: A 34-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine for 12 weeks, titrated to 350 mg nocte with good symptomatic response (PANSS reduced from 96 to 68 over the period). He had failed adequate trials of risperidone and olanzapine in the preceding two years. He attends the community clozapine clinic for his scheduled 12-week review and is accompanied by his case manager. Baseline values 12 weeks ago were weight 82 kg, BMI 26.4 kg/m2, waist 96 cm, BP 122/78 mmHg, fasting glucose 5.1 mmol/L, HbA1c 35 mmol/mol (5.4%), total cholesterol 4.6 mmol/L, LDL 2.8 mmol/L, HDL 1.1 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.4 mmol/L. Today he weighs 91 kg (BMI 29.3, waist 106 cm), BP 134/86 mmHg, fasting glucose 6.2 mmol/L, HbA1c 41 mmol/mol (5.9%), total cholesterol 5.9 mmol/L, LDL 3.6 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L. He reports no chest pain or dyspnoea. ANC today is 3.4 x 10^9/L. He is keen to continue clozapine because it has 'given him his life back'.

data_table.Baseline (week 0): Weight 82 kg, BMI 26.4, waist 96 cm, FG 5.1, HbA1c 35 (5.4%), TC 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.1, TG 1.4.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Stop clozapine immediately and switch to aripiprazole monotherapy
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of clozapine in a responder with no immediate safety crisis risks rapid psychotic relapse and rebound psychosis; the metabolic changes are significant but do not mandate immediate stop (RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Continue clozapine and add structured lifestyle intervention, metformin, and arrange cardiometabolic review; repeat metabolic panel in 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: He has gained >7% body weight (11% in 12 weeks), with new impaired fasting glucose and atherogenic dyslipidaemia. Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and AMH support continuing clozapine in a clear responder while adding lifestyle support, considering metformin (which has the best evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain), and tightening cardiometabolic monitoring; repeat panel at 4-6 weeks tracks response.

options.text: Continue clozapine unchanged at 350 mg nocte and defer the next metabolic and weight review until 12 months from baseline, treating the current panel as within expected limits for the first year of clozapine therapy
options.rationale: An 11% weight gain with new dysglycaemia and dyslipidaemia at 12 weeks meets the RANZCP threshold for intensified monitoring and active intervention, not deferral to a 12-month routine (RANZCP).

options.text: Halve the clozapine dose to 175 mg nocte to reduce metabolic load
options.rationale: Halving an effective clozapine dose risks loss of response and is not the recommended first step for metabolic adverse effects; pharmacological and lifestyle adjuncts come before dose reduction in a responder (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).

options.text: Order an urgent echocardiogram and stop clozapine pending the result
options.rationale: He has no symptoms or signs of myocarditis (no chest pain, dyspnoea, fever, tachycardia, raised troponin or CRP), so urgent echocardiogram with cessation is not indicated; the issue is metabolic, not cardiac inflammatory (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

explanation.summary: Clozapine has the strongest weight-gain and dyslipidaemia signal of any antipsychotic. In a clear responder, the framework is continue + intervene + intensify monitoring, not stop or halve (RANZCP; AMH).
key_points: >7% weight gain at 12 weeks triggers active intervention
Metformin has the strongest evidence among adjuncts for antipsychotic weight gain
Lifestyle + cardiometabolic review are mandatory, not optional
Cessation reserved for treatment failure, agranulocytosis, myocarditis, or refractory metabolic disease despite intervention
explanation.pearls: A 4-6 week reassessment after starting metformin gives an objective signal; aim to keep him on clozapine if at all possible (RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Threshold for switching antipsychotic for metabolic reasons
stem: A 41-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia has been on olanzapine 20 mg nocte for 4 years with partial symptomatic response (residual negative symptoms, no positive symptoms). She lives independently with case-management support and attends a community mental health clinic three-monthly. Over the past 3 years her weight has risen from 76 kg to 102 kg (BMI 36.1 kg/m2), waist 112 cm. Today's review shows BP 142/92 mmHg (confirmed on repeat 5 minutes apart), fasting glucose 7.2 mmol/L on two separate occasions, HbA1c 58 mmol/mol (7.5%), LDL 4.1 mmol/L, HDL 0.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.4 mmol/L. She has type 2 diabetes diagnosed 8 months ago (on metformin 1 g BD), and stage 1 hypertension (on perindopril 5 mg). She has had three trials of structured lifestyle intervention with no sustained weight loss across the past two years. She has never been on clozapine and has not had treatment-resistance formally assessed. She prefers oral medication and has a documented history of akathisia on haloperidol but tolerated a brief aripiprazole augmentation trial 3 years ago without akathisia or activation.

data_table.Comorbidities: T2DM on metformin 1 g BD; HTN on perindopril 5 mg.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next antipsychotic management step?

options.text: Continue olanzapine 20 mg and add topiramate for weight loss
options.rationale: Topiramate has modest evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain and is third-line after lifestyle and metformin; with established type 2 diabetes, frank metabolic syndrome, and a high-burden antipsychotic still onboard, the priority is to switch to a lower-burden agent rather than add a third adjunct (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Halve the olanzapine dose to 10 mg nocte, continue current diabetes and blood pressure therapy unchanged, and review weight, fasting glucose, lipids, and BP again in 3 months with no other modifications
options.rationale: Dose reduction has only weak effect on weight in established olanzapine-induced metabolic syndrome, and risks symptomatic relapse without resolving the underlying metabolic liability of the agent (AMH; RANZCP).

options.text: Switch to chlorpromazine 200 mg BD
options.rationale: Chlorpromazine is also high-burden for weight gain, sedation, and anticholinergic load, and adds postural hypotension on top of her antihypertensive; it is not a metabolically favourable switch (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Cross-taper olanzapine to aripiprazole (or lurasidone), maintain metformin, continue diabetes and BP management, monitor closely for relapse
options.rationale: She has overt metabolic syndrome with T2DM (HbA1c 7.5%) and >7% sustained weight gain on olanzapine. RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic recommend switching to a metabolically lower-burden agent (aripiprazole or lurasidone, both PBS-listed) via cross-taper, with continued antidiabetic and antihypertensive therapy and close monitoring for relapse; she previously tolerated aripiprazole.

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgery and continue olanzapine 20 mg
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery may have a role in selected cases but is not first-line ahead of switching to a metabolically favourable antipsychotic; continuing the offending agent unchanged is not appropriate (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

explanation.summary: The trigger for switching antipsychotic for metabolic reasons is sustained weight gain >7%, new or worsening dysglycaemia or dyslipidaemia, or established metabolic syndrome despite lifestyle and pharmacological adjuncts (RANZCP).
key_points: Switch agents: aripiprazole, lurasidone, ziprasidone, brexpiprazole have lowest metabolic burden
Cross-taper rather than abrupt switch to limit relapse risk
Continue diabetes and BP management through the switch
Reassess metabolic profile 6-12 weeks after switch
explanation.pearls: Document the metabolic rationale clearly - it supports later prescribing decisions and audit (RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Lithium interactions with NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, and dehydration
stem: A 56-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable on lithium carbonate 900 mg nocte for 11 years (level 0.7 mmol/L at her quarterly check three months ago), presents to her general practitioner with a 5-day history of right knee pain after a long day of gardening. Her general practitioner is considering analgesia. She also has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and indapamide 1.5 mg daily, and type 2 diabetes treated with metformin 1 g BD. She is otherwise well and has had no recent vomiting or diarrhoea. The general practitioner is aware she is on lithium and wants to choose analgesia that minimises the risk of lithium toxicity. She weighs 72 kg, eGFR was 68 mL/min/1.73m2 last month, and she drinks 1.5 L of fluid per day. She lives alone and is travelling to a coastal holiday with predicted 36-degree temperatures next week, with several days outdoors planned.

data_table.Medications: Lithium 900 mg nocte, perindopril 5 mg, indapamide 1.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD.
data_table.Recent labs: Lithium 0.7 mmol/L (3 months ago); eGFR 68; HbA1c 51 mmol/mol.

lead_in: Which analgesic plan minimises the risk of precipitating lithium toxicity?

options.text: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Non-selective NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance via prostaglandin-mediated effects on the afferent arteriole; combined with her ACE inhibitor and thiazide-like diuretic this is a classic 'triple whammy' raising the risk of acute kidney injury and lithium accumulation (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

options.text: Celecoxib 200 mg daily for 7 days
options.rationale: COX-2 selective agents also reduce renal lithium clearance and are not a safe substitute when avoiding lithium toxicity is the goal (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily with topical diclofenac
options.rationale: Naproxen interacts strongly with lithium and combining it with topical diclofenac (which has some systemic absorption) does not solve the interaction problem (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

options.text: Switch lithium to sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for one week to allow her to take ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily safely, then return to lithium at her usual maintenance dose after the analgesic course finishes
options.rationale: Abruptly switching a long-stable mood stabiliser for short-term analgesia exposes her to relapse and to valproate-related toxicities; this is not a proportionate solution (RANZCP).

options.text: Paracetamol 1 g four times daily as required, with advice to maintain fluid intake (especially during hot weather) and to avoid NSAIDs; recheck lithium level if symptoms develop or if she gets unwell
options.rationale: Paracetamol does not interact with lithium and is the safest analgesic choice. The Australian Medicines Handbook and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic emphasise avoiding NSAIDs, ACE inhibitor changes, and dehydration in patients on lithium, particularly during hot weather; she has a triple-whammy setup (ACEi + thiazide-like diuretic + lithium) and is travelling to hot weather, so hydration advice and a low threshold to recheck levels are essential.
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

explanation.summary: Lithium clearance is renal; anything that lowers eGFR (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides, dehydration) raises plasma lithium and risks toxicity (AMH).
key_points: NSAIDs (selective and non-selective) reduce renal lithium clearance
ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce lithium clearance
Thiazides reduce lithium clearance more than loop diuretics
Dehydration (heat, vomiting, diarrhoea) is the commonest precipitant of community lithium toxicity
explanation.pearls: Give every lithium patient a 'sick-day plan' covering fluids, NSAID avoidance, and when to check a level (NPS).

sources.label: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

stem: A 64-year-old man with bipolar I disorder, on lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for 14 years, is brought to a regional emergency department by his wife. Over the past 3 days he has become increasingly unsteady on his feet, with a worsening hand tremor and slurred speech. His wife says he has been 'not quite right', confused about the day of the week, and has fallen twice at home without losing consciousness. He started ramipril 5 mg daily 10 days ago for newly diagnosed hypertension and has had reduced oral intake during a recent gastroenteritis episode. His usual lithium level taken 4 months ago was 0.75 mmol/L. On arrival he is afebrile, BP 138/82, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6). He has a coarse irregular tremor, dysarthria, gait ataxia, hyperreflexia, and bilateral horizontal nystagmus. No rigidity, no clonus, pupils equal and reactive. Bloods: Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.2, urea 9.8, creatinine 132 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 48, lithium 1.6 mmol/L.

data_table.Neurological: Coarse tremor, dysarthria, ataxia, hyperreflexia, bilateral nystagmus, no rigidity, no clonus.
data_table.Bloods: Na 138, K 4.2, urea 9.8, creatinine 132 (baseline 88), eGFR 48, lithium 1.6 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which clinical feature is most specific for lithium neurotoxicity in this presentation?

options.text: Coarse tremor with hand-to-hand variation
options.rationale: A coarse tremor is a key feature but is non-specific - it can occur with multiple metabolic disturbances and other toxidromes; the combination including cerebellar and brainstem signs is more characteristic (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Cerebellar ataxia with dysarthria, nystagmus, and hyperreflexia in the context of a rising lithium level and falling renal function
options.rationale: The classic neurotoxicity picture is cerebellar (ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus) with hyperreflexia and progression toward confusion, seizures, and coma as levels rise; it is highly specific when occurring alongside a rising lithium level and acute kidney injury, as here (ramipril + dehydration + chronic lithium). Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and the Australian Medicines Handbook list this constellation as the diagnostic signature of lithium neurotoxicity.
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre: lithium toxicity

options.text: Fever above 38.5 degrees Celsius
options.rationale: Fever is not a feature of lithium toxicity and points more toward neuroleptic malignant syndrome, serotonin syndrome, or sepsis; he is afebrile (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Lead-pipe rigidity with autonomic instability
options.rationale: Lead-pipe rigidity and autonomic instability characterise neuroleptic malignant syndrome, not lithium neurotoxicity; he has no rigidity and stable observations (AMH).

options.text: Sustained inducible ankle clonus combined with ocular clonus, diaphoresis, mydriasis, and hyperactive bowel sounds in a serotonergic toxidrome pattern
options.rationale: Clonus and ocular clonus are the hallmark of serotonin syndrome, not lithium neurotoxicity (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

explanation.summary: Lithium neurotoxicity presents with cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus), hyperreflexia, tremor, and progressive cognitive impairment; fever, rigidity, and clonus point elsewhere (AMH; NSW Poisons Information).
key_points: Cerebellar + tremor + hyperreflexia = lithium neurotoxicity until proven otherwise
Precipitants: NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, diuretics, dehydration, acute kidney injury
Always check lithium level, electrolytes, renal function, ECG
Severe toxicity (level >2.5 mmol/L or refractory neurology) is an indication for haemodialysis
explanation.pearls: A 'usually-stable' lithium patient who becomes ataxic has lithium toxicity until you prove otherwise (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

sources.label: NSW Poisons Information Centre: lithium toxicity

stem: A 24-year-old woman is reviewed at a community mental health clinic 6 weeks after a hospital admission for a severe manic episode that was her first lifetime presentation. She was discharged on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily and quetiapine 200 mg nocte, with good symptomatic resolution and a return to baseline function. She lives with her partner and works part-time as an early childhood educator. She is sexually active in a stable relationship and uses condoms only as contraception. She is not currently planning pregnancy but says she might consider it in the next 2-3 years. She has no contraindication to hormonal contraception and no history of thromboembolism or migraine with aura. The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) has issued repeated safety advisories about valproate in females of reproductive potential, and the medication carries a boxed warning regarding teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental harm in exposed offspring. The treating team is reviewing whether her current prescription meets the Australian Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) framework for valproate and is asking about the next steps. She has no documented prior trials of alternative mood stabilisers.

data_table.Demographics: 24-year-old female, sexually active, condoms only, no current pregnancy plan but possibly in 2-3 years.
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 1000 mg daily, quetiapine 200 mg nocte (commenced 6 weeks ago).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step under the Australian valproate PPP framework?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate at the current 1000 mg daily dose with no change to her existing condom-only contraception; document that she has been counselled about teratogenicity in general terms and treat the issue as resolved because she is not actively trying to conceive at this clinic visit and her recent manic episode was severe enough that mood stability should be the only priority
options.rationale: The Australian valproate PPP and TGA require highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal contraception) for ALL females of reproductive potential on valproate, regardless of current pregnancy intent; condoms alone are insufficient (TGA; AMH).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Continue valproate and document that she has been counselled; defer contraception decision to her general practitioner
options.rationale: PPP responsibility sits with the prescriber; counselling without ensuring effective contraception and signed acknowledgement does not satisfy the framework (TGA; RANZCP).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Discuss switching to a non-valproate mood stabiliser (e.g., lithium or an atypical antipsychotic with mood-stabilising properties), and if valproate is retained, ensure highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal contraception), annual specialist review with signed risk acknowledgement, and a documented plan to switch before conception
options.rationale: The TGA and RANZCP guidance require that valproate in females of reproductive potential is used only when alternatives are unsuitable, with mandatory highly effective contraception, annual specialist review with signed acknowledgement of risks (foetal valproate syndrome, neural tube defects, neurodevelopmental impairment), and an explicit plan to switch before conception. Active reconsideration of alternatives is part of every review (TGA; RANZCP).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Stop valproate today without replacement and re-review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of an effective mood stabiliser after a recent manic episode risks relapse; the safer pathway is switch via overlap rather than abrupt stop (RANZCP).

options.text: Halve the valproate dose to 500 mg daily on the assumption that lower doses are not teratogenic
options.rationale: Teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental risk are present at all doses though dose-related; halving does not satisfy PPP requirements and risks loss of mood stabilisation (TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

explanation.summary: The Australian valproate PPP requires highly effective contraception, annual specialist review with signed risk acknowledgement, active consideration of alternatives, and a switch plan before conception (TGA; RANZCP).
key_points: Foetal valproate syndrome and neural tube defects in around 10% of exposed pregnancies
Neurodevelopmental impairment in around 30-40% of exposed children
Highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal contraception) is mandatory
Annual specialist review with signed acknowledgement of risks
explanation.pearls: If valproate is the only effective option, document the alternatives considered and why they were unsuitable (TGA).

sources.label: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

subtopic_detail: Foetal valproate syndrome incidence and counselling
stem: A 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been on sodium valproate 1200 mg daily for 5 years following two severe manic episodes requiring inpatient admission. She is now in stable remission for the past 30 months and consults her psychiatrist about planning pregnancy in the next 12 months. She lives with her husband and works full-time as a project manager. Previous trials of lithium (terminated due to severe cystic acne and 12 kg weight gain at therapeutic levels) and quetiapine monotherapy (failed at 600 mg nocte with breakthrough mania at 4 months) are documented in her records. She uses a copper intrauterine device for contraception and is up to date on cervical screening. She wants to understand the absolute risks of staying on valproate during pregnancy versus switching to an alternative agent, and asks specifically about the rate of foetal valproate syndrome (FVS) and major congenital malformations. Her general practitioner, treating obstetrician, and psychiatrist are jointly involved in the planning. The TGA boxed warning and Australian Pregnancy Prevention Programme materials are on the table. She wants to know what the Australian evidence actually shows about exposure-related harm.

data_table.Background: Bipolar I, stable on valproate 1200 mg/day for 5 years.
data_table.Prior trials: Lithium (severe acne, weight gain) and quetiapine 600 mg monotherapy (failed).
data_table.Contraception: Copper IUD; plans pregnancy in next 12 months.

lead_in: Which counselling statement most accurately reflects the Australian evidence on valproate exposure in pregnancy?

options.text: Valproate exposure during pregnancy carries a major congenital malformation rate of approximately 1%, which is comparable to the background population risk in Australia, and therefore continuing valproate through the planned pregnancy is reasonable without needing to organise a switch to an alternative mood stabiliser ahead of conception
options.rationale: The major malformation rate with valproate is approximately 10%, which is 3-4 times background risk, not 1% (TGA; AMH).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Risk applies only in the third trimester; first-trimester exposure is safe
options.rationale: Major structural malformations including neural tube defects arise from first-trimester exposure during organogenesis; neurodevelopmental harm extends across pregnancy. There is no safe trimester (TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily eliminates the risk of foetal valproate syndrome and major malformations
options.rationale: High-dose folate is recommended before and during early pregnancy in women on antiepileptic medications, but it does not eliminate valproate teratogenicity; it reduces some but not all neural tube defect risk (RANZCP; RANZCOG).

options.text: Valproate exposure carries about a 10% risk of major congenital malformations and a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment; switching to a lower-risk mood stabiliser before conception, with overlap to maintain mood stability, is recommended
options.rationale: TGA safety advisories and the Australian Medicines Handbook quote major malformation rates of approximately 10% (versus 2-3% background) and neurodevelopmental impairment in around 30-40% of exposed children. RANZCP recommends preconception switching to a lower-risk mood stabiliser with overlap to protect mood stability; lithium remains an option in many women despite past adverse effects, with specialist input. This is the truthful, action-relevant counselling.
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: There is no need to switch because foetal valproate syndrome is reversible after birth with early intervention
options.rationale: Foetal valproate syndrome includes irreversible structural malformations and persistent neurodevelopmental impairment; it is not reversible (TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

explanation.summary: Valproate in pregnancy carries approximately a 10% major malformation rate and 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment rate. Pre-conception switching with overlap is recommended (TGA; RANZCP).
key_points: Major malformations: about 10% (vs 2-3% background)
Neurodevelopmental impairment: 30-40% of exposed children
Neural tube defects, cardiac, craniofacial, limb anomalies all over-represented
Plan switch well before conception with overlap to maintain mood
explanation.pearls: Frame the conversation in absolute numbers, not relative risk, and document jointly with obstetrics (RANZCOG).

sources.label: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

subtopic_detail: QRS widening and sodium bicarbonate therapy
stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought to a metropolitan emergency department 90 minutes after intentional ingestion of approximately 50 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg (estimated 1.25 g). She is drowsy but rousable, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5), with mydriasis, dry mucous membranes, and warm dry skin. Observations: HR 122 sinus tachycardia, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 37.2 degrees Celsius. She has had one brief generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival, self-terminating. ECG shows sinus tachycardia, QRS 132 ms (baseline 90 ms), R wave in aVR 4 mm, terminal R/S ratio in aVR >0.7, QT 460 ms. Bloods: Na 138, K 3.8, HCO3 18, lactate 4.2, pH 7.28 on venous blood gas, paracetamol level negative. She has no prior cardiac history and is on no other medications. The team is in contact with the NSW Poisons Information Centre.

data_table.ECG: Sinus tachycardia, QRS 132 ms, R in aVR 4 mm, terminal R/S aVR >0.7, QT 460 ms.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Administer oral activated charcoal 50 g now that she is more than 90 minutes from ingestion, place her on continuous cardiac monitoring, and observe with serial ECGs every 30 minutes without any further pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Oral charcoal is contraindicated with declining GCS and unprotected airway, and observation alone misses the urgent indication for sodium bicarbonate (NSW Poisons Information Centre; Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in tricyclic overdose - it can precipitate seizures and arrhythmias by removing GABAergic protection (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Intravenous flecainide 2 mg/kg
options.rationale: Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade in tricyclic toxicity and are contraindicated; sodium bicarbonate is the antidote (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Magnesium is used for torsades de pointes from QT prolongation, but in tricyclic overdose the dominant lethal mechanism is QRS prolongation from sodium channel blockade; sodium bicarbonate addresses this directly (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg as a bolus with reassessment of QRS, with airway protection and seizure precautions, in consultation with the NSW Poisons Information Centre
options.rationale: QRS >100 ms (especially >120 ms) in tricyclic overdose predicts seizures and ventricular arrhythmias and is the classic indication for sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus (titrated to QRS narrowing and pH ~7.50-7.55). NSW Poisons Information Centre and Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology specify this pathway alongside airway protection and seizure management (benzodiazepines first-line).

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose is lethal through sodium channel blockade (QRS widening, ventricular arrhythmias) plus seizures and anticholinergic effects. Sodium bicarbonate is the antidote (NSW Poisons Information Centre).
key_points: QRS >100 ms predicts seizures; >160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmias
R wave aVR >3 mm and terminal R/S aVR >0.7 are sensitive markers
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus, titrate to QRS and pH ~7.50
Benzodiazepines for seizures; avoid flumazenil and class I antiarrhythmics
explanation.pearls: Always call NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26) early for any psychiatric medication overdose (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

subtopic_detail: Use of the NSW Poisons Information Centre pathway
stem: A 19-year-old man presents to a small rural emergency department 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of dothiepin tablets (also called dosulepin) found in his mother's medicine cabinet. He is drowsy, GCS 13, HR 118, BP 102/64, RR 16, SpO2 98%. ECG shows sinus tachycardia, QRS 108 ms (no prior baseline), R in aVR 3 mm, QT 440 ms. He has not had a seizure. Bloods are pending. The on-call rural physician is the most senior clinician on site and has not managed a tricyclic overdose in several years. The hospital has access to intravenous sodium bicarbonate, intralipid, intubation equipment, and a high-dependency bay. The nearest tertiary centre with an intensive care unit is 220 km away by road, with retrieval by aeromedical service possible. The patient's mother thinks the bottle contained 'maybe 30 to 60 tablets' but is not sure of the original count.

data_table.Setting: Rural ED, on-call physician most senior on site, ICU 220 km away.
data_table.Observations: GCS 13, HR 118, BP 102/64, ECG QRS 108 ms.
data_table.Resources: IV sodium bicarbonate, intralipid, intubation kit, HDU bay available.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to guide management decisions?

options.text: Telephone the NSW Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26 for case-specific advice (TCA risk-stratification, sodium bicarbonate thresholds, retrieval criteria), document the consult, and act on it
options.rationale: The NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26) is the national poisons information service for Australia and provides 24/7 specialist toxicology advice including TCA-specific risk stratification, indications for sodium bicarbonate, intubation, and retrieval. NSW Poisons Information Centre and Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology recommend early case-specific consultation, particularly for rural ED clinicians managing infrequent toxidromes; the advice is then documented and acted on.

options.text: Email the patient's regular general practitioner for a verified list of current medications and prior overdose history, and wait for a reply before commencing any antidote, intubation, retrieval discussion, or further pharmacological treatment, given that exact dose information would be useful before intervention
options.rationale: Waiting for an email response delays time-critical decisions; toxicology advice is available immediately by phone (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

options.text: Search a general medical app and apply the first dose suggestion found
options.rationale: General reference apps may not reflect Australian-specific advice, and clinician-to-toxicologist consultation captures case nuances apps cannot (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: Discharge home with mental health follow-up because QRS is under 120 ms
options.rationale: Discharge of any symptomatic TCA ingestion is not appropriate; QRS can progress, the dose is uncertain, and a minimum 6-hour observation with serial ECGs is standard (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

options.text: Administer intralipid 20% empirically before further assessment
options.rationale: Intralipid is reserved for refractory cardiovascular collapse from local anaesthetic or some lipophilic drug toxicities; it is not first-line in stable TCA overdose and would not be given without specialist toxicology guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

explanation.summary: The NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26) is the Australian 24/7 toxicology phone service that supports rural and metropolitan clinicians; early consultation is the standard of care for any significant overdose (NSW Poisons Information Centre).
key_points: 13 11 26 covers all of Australia, 24/7, free to caller
Use early - the advice helps with disposition and antidote thresholds
Document the consult ID and recommendations
Retrieval decisions for rural patients are part of the consult
explanation.pearls: Save 13 11 26 on every ED resus phone; it costs nothing to call too early (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

stem: A 37-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine 400 mg nocte for 8 months, registered with an Australian clozapine monitoring service (e.g. CPMS or ClopineCentral). His weekly bloods through weeks 1-18 were unremarkable; he is now on 4-weekly haematological monitoring per the standard Australian schedule. Today's FBC: Hb 142, WCC 4.8, ANC 1.6 x 10^9/L (last month 2.4), platelets 218. He has no fever, no sore throat, no mouth ulcers, and no other infective symptoms. He has not started new medications and has had no recent vaccinations. He is otherwise well, of European background (not associated with benign ethnic neutropenia), and has no prior neutropenia.
lead_in: Which combination of zone classification, dose action, monitoring frequency and registry action best fits the Australian clozapine haematological monitoring framework?

options.text: Green zone (ANC >= 2.0) - continue clozapine, continue 4-weekly bloods, no registry action
options.rationale: ANC 1.6 is below the Australian green-zone threshold (>=2.0) and triggers amber-zone management; this option misclassifies the result.

options.text: Amber zone (ANC 1.5-1.99) - continue clozapine at current dose, increase haematological monitoring to twice weekly, notify the registered clozapine monitoring service so the patient's status is updated
options.rationale: ANC 1.6 falls within the standard Australian amber zone (1.5 to <2.0); the appropriate response is to continue clozapine, increase FBC frequency to at least twice weekly until the count recovers to green, and notify the registry. There is no fever, sore throat or other sepsis flag mandating immediate clozapine cessation, and amber-zone management is well-established to avoid premature loss of a uniquely effective drug in TRS.

options.text: Red zone (ANC <1.5) - stop clozapine immediately, admit, notify registry, never rechallenge
options.rationale: ANC 1.6 is amber, not red; immediate cessation is the wrong threshold response and would unnecessarily strip a TRS patient of the only drug shown effective for him. Rechallenge policy is also more nuanced than 'never'.

options.text: Defer the next FBC by 4 weeks at the routine visit because he is clinically well
options.rationale: Anti-pattern: any drop below green requires accelerated monitoring; routine 4-weekly is unsafe at ANC 1.6 because of the risk of progression to red zone.

options.text: Continue clozapine and add lithium 250 mg nocte to push the ANC up
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation to raise neutrophil count is not standard of care and is not the appropriate response to amber-zone neutropenia; correct response is frequency escalation and registry notification, not pharmacological tampering.

explanation.summary: Australian clozapine ANC zones: green >=2.0, amber 1.5-1.99, red <1.5 x 10^9/L. Amber means continue + intensify monitoring; red means stop and discuss with haematology (AMH).
key_points: Weekly haematological monitoring for 18 weeks, then 4-weekly thereafter
Amber zone (1.5-1.99): continue, weekly bloods until green
Red zone (<1.5): stop clozapine, urgent specialist review
Benign ethnic neutropenia and BEN-modified thresholds may apply with haematology input
explanation.pearls: Always plot the ANC trajectory - a rapid fall is more concerning than a stable amber value (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

stem: A 41-year-old woman with treatment-resistant schizophrenia is admitted to a regional medical ward with community-acquired pneumonia. She has been on clozapine 350 mg nocte for 6 years with good symptomatic control and is otherwise stable in the community. She was febrile and confused at home and did not take her clozapine for the past 3 days (last dose 72 hours ago) because she misunderstood her medication routine while unwell. On admission she is afebrile after IV ceftriaxone, alert, oriented, and mentally stable, with a clear plan to continue clozapine after discussion with her usual community psychiatrist. The medical team asks the on-call psychiatry registrar how to resume clozapine safely. Her usual ANC has been in the green zone and today is 4.1 x 10^9/L. Her observations on the ward today are stable with BP 118/72 lying and 110/68 standing, HR 84, temperature 36.8. Her FBC, troponin, CRP (other than the pneumonia-related rise), and ECG are otherwise unremarkable. She has no recent change in smoking status and continues her usual 10 cigarettes per day. She has no documented prior episode of clozapine cessation since starting it 6 years ago, and no record of myocarditis on baseline echocardiography. Her case manager has confirmed adherence at home was previously reliable until this acute febrile illness.

data_table.Background: Clozapine 350 mg nocte for 6 years; last dose 72 hours ago.
data_table.Today: Afebrile, mentally stable, ANC 4.1 x 10^9/L, troponin normal, CRP elevated from pneumonia.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate clozapine resumption strategy?

options.text: Restart at her usual 350 mg nocte tonight
options.rationale: After more than 48 hours without clozapine, tolerance to hypotension, sedation, and seizure thresholds is lost; restarting at full dose risks acute orthostatic collapse, sedation, and rarely seizure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).

options.text: Restart at half her usual dose (175 mg) tonight and increase to full dose tomorrow
options.rationale: Halving then jumping to full the next night skips the standard retitration over several days and still risks haemodynamic compromise (AMH).

options.text: Stop clozapine entirely and switch to risperidone 4 mg
options.rationale: She has been a clear long-term clozapine responder; switching off because of a brief missed-dose period is not justified and risks loss of symptom control (RANZCP).

options.text: Restart at 12.5 mg tonight and retitrate over several days back to her maintenance dose, with observation for hypotension and sedation
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and the Australian Medicines Handbook state that after >=48 hours of missed clozapine, tolerance is lost and the medication must be retitrated as if starting again - typically 12.5 mg on day 1, with stepwise increases over several days back to maintenance, with monitoring of BP, sedation, and pulse. The current 72-hour gap mandates this approach.

options.text: Restart at her usual full 350 mg total daily dose tonight but split the administration into 6-hourly divided doses (87.5 mg every 6 hours) to avoid peak effects on blood pressure and sedation
options.rationale: Splitting the dose does not address tolerance loss; the total daily exposure is still excessive after 72 hours off and remains unsafe (AMH).

explanation.summary: Missing clozapine for 48 hours or more results in loss of tolerance to hypotensive and sedative effects; retitration from 12.5 mg is mandatory (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
key_points: Less than 48 hours missed: usually resume at usual dose
>=48 hours missed: restart at 12.5 mg and retitrate
Monitor BP (lying and standing), pulse, sedation, temperature
Acute illness, infection, and dehydration accentuate adverse effects
explanation.pearls: Document the missed-dose interval precisely; the 48-hour threshold is hard, not soft (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

subtopic_detail: Smoking cessation and clozapine level rise
stem: A 45-year-old man has been on clozapine 450 mg nocte for 9 years, with steady-state clozapine level 480 mcg/L (therapeutic range 350-600) measured 6 months ago. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day. He is admitted overnight to a smoke-free general medical ward for a 5-day stay following a mechanical fall, and is offered nicotine replacement therapy (patch 21 mg/24 h plus PRN lozenges) but is not yet enrolled in a structured cessation programme. He has no current symptoms of clozapine toxicity (no sedation change, no hypersalivation increase, no tachycardia, no constipation worsening). The ward team contacts the community psychiatry team for guidance on clozapine dosing during the smoke-free inpatient period and any planned post-discharge cessation.

data_table.Background: Clozapine 450 mg nocte for 9 years; smokes 25/day.

lead_in: Which is the single best summary of the relevant pharmacokinetic mechanism and the appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Smoking induces CYP3A4; cessation will halve clozapine levels and require a dose increase of 25 percent
options.rationale: Wrong enzyme and wrong direction. Clozapine is metabolised primarily by CYP1A2 (with CYP3A4 minor); polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in tobacco smoke - not nicotine - induce CYP1A2.

options.text: Nicotine itself is the inducer; full nicotine replacement therapy on admission prevents any clozapine level change, so no dose adjustment or level monitoring is required
options.rationale: Anti-pattern: nicotine is NOT the CYP1A2 inducer in this interaction (PAHs are). NRT replaces nicotine for craving control but does not replace PAH-mediated enzyme induction; clozapine levels will still rise on cessation regardless of NRT.

options.text: PAHs in tobacco smoke induce CYP1A2; abrupt cessation can raise clozapine levels by approximately 50-70 percent over 1-2 weeks. For this brief 5-day smoke-free inpatient stay, monitor for toxicity (sedation, hypersalivation, tachycardia, seizures), consider a level at day 4-5, and plan a pre-emptive ~25 percent dose reduction with level monitoring if he commits to sustained cessation post-discharge
options.rationale: Mechanism: PAHs (not nicotine) induce CYP1A2 in heavy smokers; on cessation, induction reverses with a half-life of ~1 week, so clozapine clearance falls and levels can rise 50-70 percent. For a 5-day forced abstinence with planned return to smoking, monitor toxicity and check a level. For sustained cessation, a pre-emptive ~25 percent dose reduction with serial levels is recommended (RANZCP and AMH).

options.text: Smoking inhibits CYP1A2; cessation will lower clozapine levels and require a dose increase
options.rationale: Direction reversed: smoking INDUCES CYP1A2 (it does not inhibit); cessation raises levels.

options.text: There is no clinically significant interaction between smoking and clozapine metabolism
options.rationale: False; this interaction is one of the most clinically important PAH-CYP1A2 interactions in psychiatry, with documented clozapine toxicity (seizures, sedation) following abrupt cessation.

explanation.summary: Smoking induces CYP1A2, the primary metaboliser of clozapine. Cessation removes induction and clozapine levels rise 50-70% over 1-2 weeks (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
key_points: PAHs in smoke (not nicotine) drive CYP1A2 induction
Cessation = level rise; resumption = level fall
Plan a dose review and clozapine level on day 7-14 of cessation
Same pharmacokinetic shift applies to olanzapine, theophylline, ropinirole
explanation.pearls: Inpatient smoke-free admissions are a classic, under-recognised trigger for clozapine toxicity (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

stem: A 78-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter with a 4-day history of progressive lethargy, unsteadiness, two falls, and nausea. She was started on sertraline 50 mg daily 12 days ago for moderate depressive disorder after the death of her husband. She also takes amlodipine 5 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg, and atorvastatin 20 mg. She lives alone, drinks 2-3 cups of weak tea daily and about 1 L of water. On examination she is afebrile, BP 138/76 (lying) and 124/72 (standing), HR 78, RR 14, SpO2 97%, GCS 14 (mildly drowsy but oriented), no focal neurological signs, mucous membranes moist, JVP normal, no oedema. Bloods: Na 121 mmol/L (baseline 138 four weeks ago), K 3.8, urea 4.1, creatinine 72 (baseline 70), glucose 5.4, TSH 2.0, serum osmolality 254 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 412 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 48 mmol/L. ECG shows sinus rhythm, QTc 432 ms.

data_table.Medications: Sertraline 50 mg (12 days), amlodipine 5 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg, atorvastatin 20 mg.
data_table.Bloods: Na 121 (baseline 138), K 3.8, urea 4.1, Cr 72, serum osm 254, urine osm 412, urine Na 48.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely mechanism of this hyponatraemia, and the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Thiazide-induced hyponatraemia alone is the sole mechanism; continue sertraline 50 mg daily because she has only been on it 12 days and a clinical antidepressant response is still being established, stop hydrochlorothiazide only, restrict free water intake to 1 L per day, and recheck sodium in 24 hours with no change to her antidepressant regimen
options.rationale: Thiazide certainly contributes (and should be stopped) but the temporal link (12 days after sertraline start, with euvolaemic biochemical SIADH pattern) implicates sertraline as well, and continuing it risks ongoing hyponatraemia (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

options.text: Primary polydipsia; restrict fluid intake aggressively to <500 mL/day
options.rationale: Her fluid intake is modest; biochemistry (high urine osmolality 412 with low serum osmolality 254 and urine Na 48) fits SIADH, not primary polydipsia (which would show dilute urine) (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

options.text: Addisonian crisis; give intravenous hydrocortisone and broad fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: There is no hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation, or other Addisonian features; aggressive saline could cause rapid sodium correction and central pontine myelinolysis if applied here (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cerebral salt wasting; treat with hypertonic saline and salt tablets
options.rationale: Cerebral salt wasting requires hypovolaemia and is usually seen after acute neurological injury; she is clinically euvolaemic with no neurological insult (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: SSRI-induced SIADH (potentiated by thiazide); stop sertraline AND hydrochlorothiazide, fluid restrict to 1-1.2 L/day, recheck sodium in 6-12 hours with a planned correction rate not exceeding 8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours, and consider an alternative antidepressant (e.g., mirtazapine) once sodium has normalised
options.rationale: The biochemistry (low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine, urine sodium >30 mmol/L, euvolaemia, normal TSH/cortisol pattern, recent SSRI start, concurrent thiazide) is classical SSRI-induced SIADH potentiated by thiazide. Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and the Australian Medicines Handbook advise stopping the SSRI and the thiazide, fluid restriction, controlled correction (not >8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination), and switching to an antidepressant with lower hyponatraemia risk such as mirtazapine.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

explanation.summary: SSRI-induced SIADH is most common in older women, often within 2-4 weeks of starting, and is potentiated by thiazides and low body weight (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
key_points: Check sodium at baseline and 2-4 weeks in older patients starting an SSRI
Stop SSRI + offender (thiazide), fluid restrict, controlled correction
Do not correct sodium faster than 8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours
Mirtazapine has the lowest hyponatraemia signal among antidepressants
explanation.pearls: Falls in an elderly woman recently started on an SSRI? Check the sodium before anything else (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

subtopic_detail: Citalopram QT cap and discriminating serotonin syndrome from neuroleptic malignant syndrome
stem: A 69-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his wife found him agitated, sweaty, and shaking in the lounge room of their home. Over the past 24 hours he has become progressively tremulous, restless, and confused, with deteriorating mental state through the morning. His usual medications include citalopram (recently up-titrated 5 days ago from 20 mg to 40 mg daily for persistent depressive symptoms despite a previously adequate response), risperidone 2 mg nocte (commenced 6 weeks ago for new psychotic features overlaid on depression), perindopril 5 mg daily for hypertension, and tramadol 50 mg up to four times daily started 4 days ago by an after-hours general practitioner for an acute lower back strain. On arrival he is restless and agitated, GCS 13, temperature 38.6 degrees Celsius, HR 124 sinus, BP 158/96, RR 22, SpO2 97% on room air. Examination shows mydriasis, profuse diaphoresis, hyperactive bowel sounds, tremor more pronounced in the lower limbs than the upper limbs, spontaneous sustained inducible clonus in both ankles (right > left), ocular clonus on lateral gaze, and globally brisk reflexes. He is NOT rigid - tone is normal with no lead-pipe rigidity and no cogwheeling. ECG: sinus tachycardia, QTc 502 ms (baseline 6 months ago 432 ms). Bloods: Na 138, K 4.2, creatinine 86, CK 412 U/L, lactate 2.6, paracetamol level negative.

data_table.Medications: Citalopram 40 mg (up from 20 mg, 5 days ago), risperidone 2 mg (stable 6 weeks), perindopril 5 mg, tramadol 50 mg QID (4 days).
data_table.Examination: Diaphoresis, hyperactive bowel sounds, lower-limb-predominant tremor, sustained inducible ankle clonus (R>L), ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, NO rigidity.
data_table.Bloods/ECG: Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 86, CK 412, lactate 2.6, QTc 502 ms (baseline 432).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnosis and immediate management combination?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from citalopram (above the over-65 dose cap of 20 mg) plus tramadol; stop citalopram and tramadol immediately, give benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus, supportive cooling and IV fluids, consult NSW Poisons Information Centre, monitor QT and CK, and consider cyproheptadine in refractory cases
options.rationale: His features (clonus, especially inducible and spontaneous lower-limb ankle clonus, ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds, diaphoresis, fever, lower-limb predominance, NO rigidity) meet Hunter criteria for serotonin syndrome. The trigger is the 40 mg citalopram dose (which exceeds the TGA over-65 cap of 20 mg due to dose-dependent QT prolongation and is reflected here in QTc 502 ms) combined with newly added tramadol (serotonergic). Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic, the AMH, and the NSW Poisons Information Centre recommend stopping serotonergic agents, benzodiazepine sedation, cooling, IV fluids, ECG/CK monitoring, and cyproheptadine for refractory cases.
source_refs: TGA: Citalopram and QT prolongation - safety advisory

options.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome triggered by risperidone (commenced 6 weeks ago) is the diagnosis; stop risperidone immediately, give intravenous dantrolene and oral bromocriptine as specific antidotes, provide aggressive supportive care with active cooling and IV fluids, and continue citalopram and tramadol since the toxidrome is dopamine-blockade-mediated rather than serotonergic in origin
options.rationale: NMS classically presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and very high CK (often >1000 U/L), with a slower onset; he has no rigidity, modest CK rise, lower-limb-predominant tremor with clonus, and the timing is locked to citalopram up-titration and tramadol introduction. The picture is serotonin syndrome (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - neuroleptic malignant syndrome

options.text: Sepsis with delirium; broad-spectrum antibiotics and intravenous fluids only
options.rationale: Lactate is mildly raised and there is no clear source; the focal clonus and ocular clonus point strongly to serotonin syndrome rather than sepsis. Missing the serotonergic toxidrome risks ongoing exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

options.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome from tramadol; give physostigmine
options.rationale: Anticholinergic toxidrome features dry skin and dry mucous membranes, urinary retention, and absent bowel sounds; he is diaphoretic with hyperactive bowel sounds, which excludes anticholinergic syndrome (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

options.text: Acute ischaemic stroke recurrence; thrombolyse within the window
options.rationale: There are no focal neurological deficits; the symmetric clonus, fever, and autonomic features are not consistent with stroke. Thrombolysis would be wholly inappropriate (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

explanation.summary: Two key Australian safety items combine here: the TGA citalopram dose cap of 20 mg in adults over 65 (driven by dose-dependent QT prolongation), and the serotonin syndrome triad of mental status change, neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia), and autonomic hyperactivity (TGA; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).
key_points: Citalopram max in adults over 65: 20 mg daily (TGA QT advisory)
Serotonin syndrome: clonus (especially ocular and inducible/lower-limb), hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds; NO rigidity
NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, very high CK; slower onset; antipsychotic trigger
Tramadol is a frequent co-trigger via serotonergic activity
Management: stop offenders, benzodiazepines, cooling, IV fluids, NSW Poisons Information Centre consult, cyproheptadine if refractory
explanation.pearls: Lower-limb-predominant clonus with normal tone is the textbook serotonin-syndrome physical sign; lead-pipe rigidity points the other way to NMS (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

sources.label: TGA: Citalopram and QT prolongation - safety advisory
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - neuroleptic malignant syndrome
